coredata.dc:title: Designing and evaluation of a novel electrochemical biosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold core-shell to detect and measure Human T-lymphotropic Virus-1 (HTLV-1) in clinical samples
coredata.dc:creator: Zibadi, Farkhonde
Rezayi, Majid
Kazemi Oskuee, Reza
Bolourinezhad, Monireh
Darroudi, Majid
Taghdisi, Seyed Mohammad
Radfar, Sasan
coredata.dc:description: The objective of this investigation is to introduce an innovative electrochemical biosensor that can swiftly detect HTLV-1 DNA targets without the need for nucleic acid amplification. The biosensor is equipped with a highly precise antisense DNA oligonucleotide. Additionally, we introduce a new core–shell material, CQDs@AuNPs, for the surface modification of glassy carbon electrodes. L-Cysteine is utilized as a conductive bridge to enhance the binding capabilities, electron transfer efficiency, and conductivity. The biosensor incorporates methylene blue (MB) as an electrochemical indicator with differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique, facilitating swift detection within a mere 20 min. Furthermore, it has been able to attain a detection limit of 0.7 aM and quantitation limit of 6 copies/µL with an impressive linear range spanning from 10 aM to 100 nM that displays a positive slope along with exhibiting a remarkably high correlation coefficient measuring at approximately 0.99. We tested the biosensor using 40 HTLV-1 DNA real clinical samples, comprising 30 positive and 10 negative samples, as determined by standard RT-PCR. The biosensor demonstrated a specificity of 96.67% and an exceptional sensitivity of 100%. Rigorous quantitative analysis and statistical techniques, including t-tests, cutoff value determination, receiver operating characteristic curves, and box diagrams, clearly differentiated between the positive and negative groups. These findings underscore the potential of our innovative biosensor as a rapid, precise, and cost-effective tool for early detection of HTLV-1, which holds promise for efficient management and control of the virus.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Human T-lymphotropic virus type 1
Electrochemical biosensor
HTLV-1 DNA detection
Methylene blue
CQDs@Au core–shell
Differential pulse voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the biosensor
The efficiency of the biosensor in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support for this study was provided by Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (MUMS) under grant number 992040. The content of this report is derived from the MSc thesis of Farkhonde Zibadi.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (A), FE-SEM images of CQDs@Au/GCE (B) and bare GCE (C), TEM image in different scales (D) and (E), EDX analysis and corresponding elemental mapping data of CQDs@Au (F).

(A) CVs of different modified electrodes in 5.0 mM [Fe (CN)6]-3/-4 containing 0.5 M KCl, (a) blank PBS, (b) bare GCE, (c) CQDs@Au/GCE, (d) ssDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE, (e) dsDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE. (B) EIS of different modified electrodes in 5.0 mM [Fe (CN)6]-3/-4 containing 0.5 M KCl, (a) bare GCE, (b) CQDs@Au/GCE, (c) ssDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE, (d) dsDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE (C) CVs of bare GCE and, (D) CQDs@Au/GCE at different scan rate (0.01 to 0.15 mV/s), (E) Linear curves of bare GCE and CQDs@Au/GCE in 5.0 mM [Fe (CN)6]-3/-4 containing 0.5 M KCl at different scan rates (0.01 to 0.15 mV/s). AFM images of (F) bare GCE, (G) CQDs@Au/GCE, (H) ssDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE, (I) dsDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE.

(A) Sensitivity analysis of the synthetic target with DPV technique in the 10–1011 aM range applying MB as redox agent. (B) The generated log [C] vs. DPV calibration plots. (C) DPV signals of selectivity using synthetic targets with 5 and 3 mismatches and non-complementary sequences. (D) The stability of final sensing platform for 1 pM synthetic target during 30 days using the DPV technique. (E) Reproducibility of developed biosensor by using four identical CQDs@Au /GCE were tested for 1 pM synthetic target.

(A) Box-diagram for the comparison of quantitative RT-PCR and DPV detection of HTLV-1 DNA in 40 clinical samples. (B) ROC analysis and (C) T-test to evaluate the biosensor's detection performance. (D) Selectivity investigation using different subtypes of HIV, (HIV-1, HIV-2) and HBV DNAs from positive clinical samples. The error bars represent the detection limit viations measured by three independent measurements. (E) Sensitivity assessment using DNA obtained from real clinical samples with a copy number in the range of 10–108 copies/L. (F) The resulting calibration plots for log copies/µL.
(A) Synthesis of CQDs and CQDs@Au (B) Electrodeposition, immobilization of the probe, and electrode preparation (C) Electrochemical detection employing turn-on techniques.
The comparison our work with the prior techniques for HTLV-1 detection.

row.entry: Magnetic beads, Cy3 and Cy5 fluorophores

head.ce:title: Designing and evaluation of a novel electrochemical biosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold core-shell to detect and measure Human T-lymphotropic Virus-1 (HTLV-1) in clinical samples

Targeted Drug Delivery Research Center, Pharmaceutical Technology Institute, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran (S. Mohammad Taghdisi).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The objective of this investigation is to introduce an innovative electrochemical biosensor that can swiftly detect HTLV-1 DNA targets without the need for nucleic acid amplification. The biosensor is equipped with a highly precise antisense DNA oligonucleotide. Additionally, we introduce a new core–shell material, CQDs@AuNPs, for the surface modification of glassy carbon electrodes. L-Cysteine is utilized as a conductive bridge to enhance the binding capabilities, electron transfer efficiency, and conductivity. The biosensor incorporates methylene blue (MB) as an electrochemical indicator with differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique, facilitating swift detection within a mere 20 min. Furthermore, it has been able to attain a detection limit of 0.7 aM and quantitation limit of 6 copies/µL with an impressive linear range spanning from 10 aM to 100 nM that displays a positive slope along with exhibiting a remarkably high correlation coefficient measuring at approximately 0.99.

We tested the biosensor using 40 HTLV-1 DNA real clinical samples, comprising 30 positive and 10 negative samples, as determined by standard RT-PCR. The biosensor demonstrated a specificity of 96.67% and an exceptional sensitivity of 100%. Rigorous quantitative analysis and statistical techniques, including t-tests, cutoff value determination, receiver operating characteristic curves, and box diagrams, clearly differentiated between the positive and negative groups. These findings underscore the potential of our innovative biosensor as a rapid, precise, and cost-effective tool for early detection of HTLV-1, which holds promise for efficient management and control of the virus.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: The initial retrovirus found to be oncogenic in humans was the type 1 human T-cell leukemia virus (HTLV-1), which results in chronic, lifelong infection and a variety of clinical disorders. The most prevalent clinical disorders caused by HTLV-1 are Adult T-cell Leukemia (ATL) and HTLV-1 associated Myelopathy or Total Spastic Paraparesis (HAM/TSP) [1]. assessments regarding the total count of individuals infected with HTLV-1 vary between a range of 15 million to 20 million [2,3]. Most HTLV-1-infected patients do not have any symptoms. HTLV-1 can be transmitted through contaminated body fluids such as blood, breast milk, seminal fluid, and through the use of injectable drugs, causes a lifelong persistent infection that remains continuously active. The persistent and transmissible nature of HTLV-1 has led the World Health Organization (WHO) to prioritize its understanding of HTLV epidemiology and the development of effective prevention strategies. Thus, the World Health Organization strongly recommends implementing a worldwide plan focused on eliminating HTLV-1 [3]. Given the potential modes of transmission, early diagnosis is crucial to effectively control the spread of this virus.

Biochemical serological methods such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4], chemiluminescent immunoassay, and particle agglutination (PA) are commonly used for initial screening of HTLV-1 infection. Western blot (WB) method is also employed as confirmatory test. Molecular method like quantitative Real-time polymerase chain reaction (Real-time PCR) has become commonplace in the identification of the integrated provirus in peripheral blood cells, serving as a standard and reference test [5].

At present, Real-time PCR is the most efficient approach available for HTLV-1 detection, primarily due to its high sensitivity. However, Real-time PCR has certain drawbacks, such as the requirement for skilled laboratory staff and specialized equipment. Additionally, it is a time-consuming and costly process. Consequently, there arises an urgent need to develop a dependable, cost-effective, and rapid diagnostic technique that could improve the management of HTLV-1 [6]. Moreover, ELISA (enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay) has been widely recognized as a swift method for the detection of HTLV-1. False positive results can occur in immunoassays for HTLV-1 detection due to cross-reactivity with HTLV-2 type proteins, as the structural proteins of these two types are highly similar. Furthermore, immunoassays pose diagnostic obstacles due to the considerable duration between host infection and antibody generation as well as the likelihood of inadequate levels of antibodies for identification. Also, Patients in the incubation period may show false negative results with WB and other antibody-based techniques, making the reliability of these methods less consistent for detecting new infections.

Despite the challenges mentioned earlier, there is a need for the introduction of a precise, reliable, highly sensitive, speedy, and cost-efficient point-of-care (POC) diagnostic method for HTLV-1. Electrochemical biosensors have emerged as promising candidates to meet these requirements, offering exceptional sensitivity and selectivity, potential mobility, rapid assay time, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness [7]. The utilization of these biosensors has proven to be efficacious in the identification of diverse strains of viruses, such as Ebola virus (EBV) [8], human immune deficiency virus (HIV) [9], Zika virus (ZIKV) [10], hepatitis C virus (HCV) [11], and recently, SARS-CoV-2 [12], highlighting their versatility and potential for rapid and accurate point-of-care diagnosis. Additionally, the development of advanced nanomaterials and signal amplification techniques has further enhanced the performance and specificity of electrochemical biosensors [13,14].

The present research endeavors to fabricate an electrochemical biosensor with exceptional sensitivity for the precise detection of HTLV-1, thereby enabling specific diagnosis. The biosensor utilized a GCE (Glassy Carbon Electrode) reinforced by carbon quantum dots/gold nanoparticles (CQDs@Au/GCE). This innovative approach enabled the device to attain exceptional sensitivity levels, eliminating any need for nucleic acid amplification. CQDs were chosen for their remarkable properties such as conductivity, high water solubility, chemical stability, ease of surface functionalization, small size, and non-toxicity [15]. AuNPs, on the other hand, were selected for their favorable biocompatibility, excellent electrical conductivity, substantial surface area in proportion to its volume and capacity for forming Au-S chemical bonds that enable the attachment of biological constituents [16,17]. The electrode's surface area can be significantly enhanced by utilizing core–shell structures with gold and carbon quantum dots. A thin gold layer is added to the carbon quantum dot to boost its surface area effectiveness, while the carbon quantum dot core aids in connecting the gold to the electrode surface, thereby enhancing signal amplification and electron transport [18]. The characteristics of CQDs and AuNPs were utilized to amplify the electrical output of the biosensor. L-cysteine (L-Cys) was used to facilitate the immobilization of the specialized ssDNA probe targeting HTLV-1 DNA onto the modified electrode surface. L-cysteine is a powerful amino acid for modifying surfaces. It contains thiol groups that can create robust connections with gold or silver electrodes, along with carboxylic and amino groups that can interact with a range of biological substances, such as DNA [17]. This probe was created based on an investigation of HTLV-1 genomes. holds significant importance in biological systems. It serves as a crucial component of electrochemical sensor materials, offering abundant binding sites for analytes, facilitating expeditious electron transfer, and demonstrating modifiable conductivity [17,19]. The substance can serve as a conductive link connecting the analyte and electrode surface, thereby augmenting electron transfer efficacy and conductivity. This leads to increased precision in detecting target analytes along with enhanced dependability. The developed biosensor was subjected to an incubation step with a synthetic target sequence or DNA derived from patient samples, performed at room temperature for a duration of 20 min. The methylene blue (MB), an electroactive dye, and the redox indicator [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 (ferri-ferrocyanide) were used as the electrochemical probes for the differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) procedure, respectively. The surface properties and electrochemical characterization of the biosensor were investigated using AFM (atomic force microscope) and EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy). The biosensor's capacity for precise detection, selectivity, stability and reproducibility were thoroughly assessed under optimal conditions. The biosensor's ability to precisely and sensitively detect HTLV-1 in actual patient samples was demonstrated by the significant concordance between biosensor results and RT-PCR technique. Given its performance, the developed biosensor holds promise as a valuable tool for sensitive, precise, and prompt diagnosis of HTLV-1 in clinical settings.
Extensive details pertaining to the materials and instrumentation utilized can be located within the supplementary data file.
Comprehensive information regarding the synthesis of CQDs and CQDs@Au can be found in the supplementary data file.

In this study, we carefully selected a specific single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) capture probe designed to target the Tax gene sequence within the HTLV-1 genome. Numerous studies have consistently demonstrated the exceptional responsiveness of these areas in detecting and diagnosing HTLV-1 at a molecular level with precision [20]. Initially, a collection of tax sequences from various HTLV-1 genotypes was obtained from the NCBI HTLV-1 database. Subsequently, sequence alignment was performed using the ClustalW algorithm in BioEdit sequence alignment editor v7.2.5 [21] to identify highly conserved regions within the tax gene. The software version 6.0.11 of Gene Runner was employed to identify appropriate probe sequences through the assessment of optimal binding and disruption energies, while Mfold was utilized for secondary structure verification purposes [22]. Furthermore, the probe's specificity was reconfirmed through BLASTn nucleotide analysis (https://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi), ensuring its exclusive applicability for detecting the HTLV-1 virus while preserving genetic sequence information. The oligonucleotide sequences employed for this research are provided in Table S1.

Firstly, to clean the surface of the electrode and create a bare electrode, aluminum oxide was used to polish surface of the electrode, proceeded by immersion in a solution of ethanol and distilled water (1:1) and sonication for 1 min. Subsequently, the electrode underwent a thorough rinsing process with double distilled water to effectively eliminate all residual contaminants. Afterward, to achieve a stable electrode surface modification and a uniform distribution of nanoparticles to increase the surface area, the GC bare electrode was immersed in a CQDs@Au solution and underwent an electrochemical deposition process consisting of 40 cycles using a potential range of −1 V to + 2 V and a scanning rate of 50 mV/s by the cyclic voltammetry method. This resulted in the formation of CQDs/Au/GCE. Finally, a mixture of L-cys (7.5 mM) was dissolved in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) and electrodeposited onto the modified electrode through 16 cycles using a potential range of −1.5 V to + 2 V and a scanning rate of 100 mV/s. L-cysteine (HOOC-CH–(NH2)–CH2-SH) can act as a bridge for the binding of probes to Au, with −SH inherently binding to the Au and –NH2 interacting with the probe [23].

For ssDNA probe immobilization, 8 μL of 500 nM ssDNA, prepared in a PBS (0.05 M, pH 7.4), was dropped to the modified working electrode (L-cys/Au/CQDs/GCE) and kept at room temperature for 60 min. the electrode underwent a thorough washing process using phosphate buffer solution with a concentration of 0.05 M and pH level at 7.4 to effectively eliminate any unattached ssDNA probe present in the ssDNA/L-cys/Au/CQDs/GCE configuration. Then, 6 μL of 1 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) was dispensed onto the electrode and incubated at room temperature for 15 min to block any nonspecific binding sites [24].

The electrode modified with ssDNA probe (ssDNA/L-cys/CQDs@Au/GCE) was immersed in various concentrations of target ssDNA (containing PBS 0.05 M, pH 7.4) and incubated at room temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, the electrode was rinsed using PBS buffer to remove any unbound ssDNA targets.

In this study, HTLV-1 DNA was detected using the DPV technique and the turn-on strategy. A turn-on approach was created based on the observation of the electrical current produced by the MB in order to efficiently monitor the hybridization process. MB must be included in the dsDNA structure to function as a redox marker [25,26]. Upon being integrated into the DNA structure, MB undergoes a two-electron reduction, leading to the formation of leucomethylene blue and the generation of an electrical signal. This phenomenon may be attributed to MB's cationic properties, enabling it to interact electrostatically with anionic DNA, or its specific attraction to guanine nucleotides present in DNA strands [12]. After the hybridization step, the dsDNA modified electrode was immersed in 20 µM MB containing 20 mM NaCl for 20 min, and the electrode was cleaned with PBS to remove any unbound MB. DPV measurements were carried out in PBS at a scan rate of 50 mV/s within a potential range of −0.6 V to + 0.2 V. Electrochemical characterization was performed using EIS and CV measurements in 0.5 mM [Fe (CN)6]-3-4 and 0.5 M KCl. The CV curves were obtained between −0.6 V and + 0.6 V potential with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The EIS assessments were obtained with an amplitude of 10 mV and a frequency range between 10 kHz to 100 kHz. Scheme 1 illustrates the assembly procedure and sensing mechanism of the biosensors.

The nanocomposite substrate synthesized in this study was subjected to various analyses including UV–visible spectroscopy and spectrofluorimetric wavelength, zeta potential measurement, X-ray diffraction (XRD), FTIR spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The aforementioned analyses were performed with the purpose of examining various aspects such as the morphology, chemical structure, functional groups, and surface topology of the nanocomposite substrate.

UV–visible spectroscopy (Fig. S1a) was used as an initial test to confirm the synthesis of carbon quantum dots. The analysis revealed absorption peaks at 222 and 280 nm, which corresponded to the expected absorption wavelengths of carbon quantum dots. These peaks were attributed to the *π-π transition of the carbon–carbon (C = C) bond and the π-n transition of the carbonyl group (C = O), respectively [27]. The fluorescence spectrum of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Fig. S1b) was also examined, revealing excitation and emission peaks at 280 nm and 420 nm, respectively [28].

The zeta potential investigation was carried out to ascertain the electrostatic charge and stability of the nanoparticles. The results revealed a distinct peak of the CQD at −12.4 mV, and for CQDs@Au it was observed to be −25 mV, indicating a negative charge and a stable structure for these nanoparticles (Fig. S2).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) examination was conducted on both the synthesized CQDs and the CQDs@Au. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns exhibited in Fig. S3 are representative of both CQDs and CQDs@Au, and align with established data. Fig. S3 (1) demonstrates the absence of additional peaks, verifying the high purity of the synthesized compound. The XRD pattern of the CQDs@Au (Fig. S3 (2)) reveals distinct peaks at angles of 38.2°, 43.6°, 64°, and 77.2°, indicating the presence of gold (111), gold (200), gold (220), and gold (311) on the surface of the CQDs [29–31].

The chemical structure and bond types of the samples were analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. Fig. 1a presents absorption peaks detected in the phase of CQDs at 3400, 1680, 2910, and 1400 cm−1, representing the stretching vibrations of –OH, C = O, C-H, and C = C bonds, respectively. In the CQDs@Au composite analysis, absorption peaks were observed at 3300, 1680, and 1060 cm−1, indicating the stretching vibrations of –OH, C = O, and C = C bonds. The peak at 3300 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the –OH bond of water molecules adsorbed on the nanoparticle surface, confirming the successful synthesis of the sample [28,29,32].

The subsequent step involved using FE-SEM to characterize the nanoparticles on the modified electrode. As depicted in Fig. 1B, a uniform coating of CQDs@Au was successfully deposited onto the surface of GCE. This is evidenced by contrasting FE-SEM images with those obtained from the unmodified electrode (Fig. 1C). One of the most significant aspects of our developed biosensor is synthesizing core–shell particles that have a great effective surface area. Fig. 1D displays TEM images of the CQDs@Au, formed by incorporating AuNPs onto the CQDs. The images (Fig. 1 E) confirm the core–shell structure of these nanocomposites, where the CQDs serve as the core, surrounded by a thin layer of AuNPs that have grown on the surface.

Furthermore, EDAX/MAP analysis was employed to ascertain the elemental composition and quantities within the sample. Fig. 1F displays the EDAX spectrum of the synthesized sample, revealing the presence of carbon, gold, chlorine, and a minor amount of nitrogen elements.

The CV and EIS techniques were utilized to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the modified electrode surface and to monitor its electron transfer characteristics using [Fe (CN)6]-3-4 as the redox agent. To describe the EIS data, Nyquist spectra were presented. The Nyquist graph demonstrates two crucial zones for the processes occurring on the surface of the electrode. The low-frequency linear region provided information on the redox mediator's mass transport at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. In contrast, the high-frequency half-circle area indicated the mechanisms of electron transport [33].

Fig. 2 A presents the cyclic voltammograms, while Fig. 2B displays the Nyquist plots. The CV voltammograms were used with a potential range of −0.6 V to + 0.6 V. Initially, a baseline PBS peak was recorded (Fig. 2a, curve a). As shown, in comparison to the bare GCE in CV diagram (Fig. 2A, curve b) and EIS (Fig. 2B, curve a), the electrodeposit of CQDs@Au onto electrode surface, leads to a substantial enhancement in redox currents (Fig. 2A, curve c) and a notable decrease in potential difference (Fig. 2B, curve b), suggesting that the electron transfer process was enhanced as a result of broadening the functional capacity of the electrode and improving its conductivity. Upon the immobilization of DNA probes, the redox currents decrease (Fig. 2A, curve d), aligning with the increase in Rct value in EIS analysis (Fig. 2B, curve c). The rise in Rct is ascribed to the electrostatic repulsion that occurs between the deoxyribose phosphate backbone, which bears a negative charge and [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4 probes also carrying negatively charged ions. [34,35]. Consequently, the binding of DNA targets to DNA probes leads to reduced redox currents (Fig. 2A, curve e), and a greater potential difference, aligning with the rise in Rct value (Fig. 2B, curve d). Clearly, the findings from the CV and EIS analyses are in good agreement. These consistent results provide strong evidence for the successful development of the electrochemical DNA biosensor.

CV measurements were performed at a range of scan rates (0.01 to 0.15 mV/s) to evaluate the performance of both bare GCE (Fig. 2C) and modified GCE (Fig. 2D). Furthermore, the effective surface areas of both bare GCE and modified GCE were quantitatively compared using the Randle’s–Ševcik equation [36]. The obtained results, as depicted in Fig. 2E, align well with the findings and demonstrate that CQDs@Au significantly enhance the effective surface area of the electrode for the immobilization of ssDNA probes, thereby enabling improved detection capabilities. As seen, the peak currents clearly rise as the scan rate increases. The peak currents and scan rate exhibited strong linear correlation, indicating that the electrochemical reaction is diffusion-controlled [24].

Atomic force microscopy analysis (AFM) images in compare the topography of the CQDs@Au/GCE to the bare GCE electrode. The CQDs@Au modified GCE exhibited a noticeably higher roughness (40.437 nm, Fig. 2G) compared to the bare surface (13.76 nm, Fig. 2F), indicating successful surface activation. Following the immobilization of ssDNA probes, the roughness of the modified electrode surface increased to 60.591 nm, indicating the successful formation of the probes on the electrode surface (Fig. 2H). Finally, upon hybridization with ssDNA target, the roughness values exhibited an increase to 82.80 nm (Fig. 2I). These findings suggest that the probe molecules successfully interacted with the ssDNA target molecules, forming a dsDNA structure that completely covers the surface of the modified GCE [37].

To achieve optimal electrochemical results and the highest possible performance of the BSA/ssDNA/Lcys/CQDs@Au/GC electrode, such as high specificity and sensitivity, various parameters were evaluated and optimized, using the DPV technique. In the optimization process, probe and buffer concentrations, probe immobilization time, hybridization time, temperature, pH, and MB treatment time were examined.

Since the concentration of the ssDNA probe plays a critical role in determining the biosensor's performance, the probe concentration was first optimized [38]. we examined the impact of the probe concentration on the signal response within the range of 1–1000 nM. As shown in Fig. S4A, the ssDNA probe concentration of 500 nM displayed the highest redox label (MB) signal.

The duration for stabilizing the ssDNA probe on the GCE surface was investigated within the range of 30 to 120 min. As evident from the Fig. S4B, the optimal DNA immobilization time is 60 min. Consequently, subsequent tests were conducted using ssDNA probe concentration of 500 nM and an immobilization time of 60 min to ensure the maximum efficiency of the biosensor.

In order to achieve an accurate and sensitive response within a shorter timeframe of hybridization time between the ssDNA probe and ssDNA target, the performance of the biosensor was assessed at various hybridization times, ranging from 10 to 70 min in increments of 10 min. The results depicted in Fig. S4C indicate that a threshold hybridization time of 20 min is required to achieve a consistent and stable hybridization, which is notably shorter compared to many previous reports.

To determine the optimal hybridization temperature, a range of temperatures including 15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C, 45 °C, and 55 °C were tested, and the performance of the biosensor was evaluated at each temperature (Fig. S4D). The DPV diagram demonstrates the specific hybridization of the ssDNA probe and ssDNA target at room temperature, highlighting the advantageous design of this biosensor.

The concentration of the electrolyte solution is a crucial factor that significantly impacts the ultimate outcomes, necessitating comprehensive assessment at different levels [39]. To accomplish this, phosphate buffer solutions at four different concentrations (0.025, 0.050, 0.075, and 0.1 M) were prepared and diluted accordingly. Following the surface modification, probe fixation, and hybridization steps, the electrodes were immersed in these solutions, and the DPV signal was measured. The optimization results, illustrated in Fig. S4E, indicate that the concentration of 0.075 was identified as the most effective concentration for the electrolyte solution.

The graph in Fig. S4F indicates that a pH of 7.4, similar to the pH of physiological body fluids, was identified as the optimal pH for enhancing the biosensor's performance. Based on the findings from Fig. S4G it was determined that a concentration of 0.1 M buffer for preparing the DNA target solution is the optimal condition for the biosensor's performance. pH significantly influences the reaction [40], thus amperometric tests using various pH values were conducted.

As previously mentioned, our study utilizes MB as the electroactive substance. Consequently, the duration of immersing the modified and hybridized electrode, in a particular concentration of MB is of considerable importance. To investigate this, the modified electrode was exposed to the target sequence and immersed in a 20 μM solution of MB containing 20 mM sodium chloride for different durations: 10, 20, 30, and 40 min. The resulting electrochemical signal was evaluated at each time point. Fig. S4H illustrates that the optimal and most suitable immersion time in the MB solution is 20 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity, selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the detection of single-stranded target DNA was carried out using DPV testing. Various concentrations of single-stranded target DNA were tested under optimal conditions to determine the linear range of the biosensor and the lowest detectable value (LOD). DPV current was recorded for a range of target DNA concentrations, starting from 10 aM and going up to 100 nM. As the concentration of target DNA increased, an observable increase in DPV current was noted (Fig. 3 a).

According to the data presented in Fig. 3b, it can be inferred that a linear correlation exists between the oxidation current of MB and the logarithmic value of DNA target concentration within a specified range from 10 aM up to 100 nM. By fitting the data with a linear equation, the corresponding peak current (Ip, μA) was determined to follow the equation Ip (μ A) = 1.4572 LogC + 33.931. The obtained correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99) indicates a strong and acceptable linear correlation between the variables. Also, our biosensor demonstrated an impressive 0.7 aM detection limit, showcasing its exceptional sensitivity in detecting HTLV-1 DNA targets at extremely low concentrations. For this purpose, used the LOD = 3.3(Sy/S) formula [41,42], in accordance with the guidelines provided by IUPAC [43].

Table 1 provides a comprehensive comparison of various techniques utilized for the diagnosis of HTLV-1, highlighting their respective performances. The findings of this comprehensive examination unequivocally indicate that the biosensor presented herein surpasses another biosensor in relation to its expansive linear range and lower LOD for quantitative HTLV-1 analysis.

To assess the selectivity, single-stranded synthetic oligonucleotides were utilized, including the target sequence with three-mismatches, five-mismatches, and a non-complementary sequence (all the mentioned DNA sequences were prepared at a concentration of 1 pM and the process was conducted under optimized conditions). The findings of the experiment are illustrated in Fig. 3c. When the ssDNA-probe is placed alone on the electrode surface, no current is observed since no hybridization occurs. However, when the ssDNA-probe and the ssDNA target are hybridized, a high DPV current is observed due to the oxidation of MB trapped within the double-stranded DNA. In the case of the ssDNA target with three-mismatches, the DPV current is reduced because the hybridization is not complete. Likewise, for the ssDNA target having five mismatches, there is an even greater degree of incomplete hybridization, leading to a lower peak in DPV current. Finally, when non-complementary DNA is used, which cannot pair with the ssDNA-probe, no DPV signal is observed.

The stability of the biosensors was evaluated by preparing the modified GC electrode for hybridization with a concentration of 1 pM ssDNA target. After storing the biosensors at + 4 °C in the refrigerator for 30 days, it was found that the biosensors retained approximately 85 % of their initial DPV current (Fig. 3d). This level of retention can be considered acceptable and indicates satisfactory stability for the biosensors. Relative standard deviation was 3 % that indicates good stability of our designed biosensor.

The reproducibility of the DNA biosensor was assessed by conducting DPV current measurements using a modified GCE and a 1 pM ssDNA target under identical conditions. This evaluation was performed on four separate GCE, and the obtained DPV currents for the three repetitions were 40.3, 37.8, 40.5 and 40.4 respectively. Calculating the RSD based on these values yielded a value of 3.2 %, indicating a good level of reproducibility and confirming the acceptable performance of the biosensor's DNA component (Fig. 3e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The efficiency of the biosensor in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the efficiency of the designed biosensor was assessed using a total of 40 samples. The Institutional Ethical Committee and Research Advisory Committee of Mashhad University of Medical Sciences granted approval for experiments (IR.MUMS.MEDICAL.REC.1400.080). This included 10 negative samples and 30 DNA samples extracted from human PBMC (peripheral blood mononuclear cell), which were obtained from the patients who referred to the central laboratory of Ghaem Hospital (Mashhad, Iran). These samples were confirmed as negative and positive for HTLV-1 virus infection using real-time PCR. The biosensor (BSA/ssDNA/Lcys/CQDs@Au/GCE) was tested under optimal conditions, using a phosphate buffer solution of 0.075 M and pH 7.4. The response of the biosensor was then evaluated for these samples. The biosensor's response to the 10 negative samples, as confirmed by real-time PCR, yielded a negative result with an electrochemical current intensity close to zero. This is attributed to the absence of hybridization reaction in these samples, as they do not contain viral DNA. On the other hand, the biosensor exhibited a positive response when measuring the 30 positive samples confirmed by real-time PCR. The observed electrochemical current in these samples was attributed to the presence of viral DNA and the successful hybridization reaction with the complementary oligonucleotide probe on the biosensor substrate. These results demonstrate the high efficiency of the DNA biosensor in detecting the HTLV-1 virus in real extracted human DNA samples. In addition, a comparative analysis was conducted to evaluate the biosensor's ability in detecting real samples by examining both positive and negative responses. This involved creating a chart that correlated CT values obtained from real-time PCR with electrochemical currents recorded for all 40 samples. The chart can be observed in Fig. 4 A. ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curves (Fig. 4B) and t-test (Fig. 4C) were conducted to assess the significant difference between the mean values of the independent positive and negative groups. The biosensor achieved an AUC (Area Under Curve) value of 1, based on these findings, the designed biosensor exhibits high specificity and selectivity (97 % specificity and 100 % sensitivity). The results of this test indicate the positive and negative groups differed significantly from each other, with a probability value (p-value) of 0.0001 based on the t-distribution. These tests suggest a threshold limit of 32.40 based on the IUPAC technique, whereby samples exhibiting a DPV intensity higher than 32.40 A are classified as positive samples, while samples with a DPV current intensity lower than 32.40 A are classified as negative samples [51].

In order to evaluate the precision and accuracy of the biosensor that was created, authentic clinical samples were utilized. These included two PBMC extracts contaminated with HIV as well as one sample infected with HBV, which were all confirmed positive through real-time PCR analysis. These samples were directly supplied to the biosensor under optimal conditions, without any additional preparation. The biosensor's response was evaluated, and Fig. 4D illustrates the results. Interestingly, the biosensor showed a negative response to the HIV and HBV samples, with the resulting electrochemical current intensity close to zero. This indicates the absence of a hybridization reaction between these samples and the biosensor. On the other hand, the HTLV-1 positive sample exhibited the highest electrochemical current intensity among all the samples tested.

To assess the sensitivity of the designed biosensor using real clinical samples, 8 dilutions of a real sample were prepared based on the number of virus copies and the standard curve. These diluted samples were then analyzed using the biosensor under optimal conditions. The resulting electrochemical signals were recorded, and a calibration graph was plotted (Fig. 4E). Fig. 4F demonstrates a linear relationship between the intensity of the electrochemical current and the logarithm of the diluted DNA sequences based on the number of copies. The line equation, y = 1.2785x + 43.058, with an R-squared value of 0.99, was obtained. Additionally, the biosensor achieved a quantitation limit of 6 copies/µL (using LOQ = 10(Sy/S) formula), signifying its ability to accurately quantify the presence of HTLV-1 DNA in clinical samples. This finding highlights the biosensor's ability to detect small amounts of the virus sequence in the early stages of the disease, enabling quick and timely diagnosis. In order to assess the biosensor's performance more effectively, the sensitivity of the biosensor was evaluated using serum matrices. Serum is used to evaluate biosensor sensitivity because it contains various biomarkers and analytes that can provide valuable information about the performance of the biosensor [52]. In conclusion, a comparative analysis was conducted between the outcomes acquired in serum matrix and PBS (Table S2). These findings underscore the efficiency of the biosensor in identifying objective analytes across diverse matrices, particularly within serum samples exhibiting an insignificant viral load measuring 6 copies/µL.

In this study, we developed a novel platform consisting of ssDNA immobilized on CQDs@Au, which effectively and rapidly evaluated HTLV-1 DNA isolated from real clinical samples. Notably, the biosensor demonstrated accurate detection without the need for nucleic acid amplification, streamlining the diagnostic process.

The DNA biosensor exhibited an impressive 0.7 aM detection limit, highlighting its exceptional sensitivity in detecting HTLV-1 DNA targets at extremely low concentrations. Moreover, the biosensor achieved a quantitation limit of 6 copies/µL, showcasing its ability to precisely quantify the presence of HTLV-1 DNA in clinical samples. The extensive linear range spanning from 10 aM to 100 nM amplifies the biosensor's utility, guaranteeing that it does not reach saturation expeditiously while undergoing testing. The aforementioned characteristics render it a highly auspicious instrument for the timely detection and surveillance of HTLV-1 infections, including during the early phases of viral development.

In conclusion, our biosensor demonstrated promising performance in detecting HTLV-1, offering a potential solution for rapid and sensitive diagnosis. The successful construction of the biosensor, supported by DPV, CV, and EIS results, confirms its feasibility and reliability for practical applications.

Farkhonde Zibadi: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Majid Rezayi: . Reza Kazemi Oskuee: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Monireh Bolourinezhad: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Majid Darroudi: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Seyed Mohammad Taghdisi: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Sasan Radfar: Investigation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support for this study was provided by Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (MUMS) under grant number 992040. The content of this report is derived from the MSc thesis of Farkhonde Zibadi.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110563.

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot-based biosensor for the detection of human T-lymphotropic virus-1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Norouzi, M. Zarei Ghobadi, M. Golmimi, S.-H. Mozhgani, H. Ghourchian, S.A. Rezaee, Quantum dot-based biosensor for the detection of human T-lymphotropic virus-1, Analytical Letters, 50 (2017) 2402-2411.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A genosensor for detection of HTLV-I based on photoluminescence quenching of fluorescent carbon dots in presence of iron magnetic nanoparticle-capped Au

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zarei-Ghobadi, S.-H. Mozhgani, F. Dashtestani, A. Yadegari, F. Hakimian, M. Norouzi, H. Ghourchian, A genosensor for detection of HTLV-I based on photoluminescence quenching of fluorescent carbon dots in presence of iron magnetic nanoparticle-capped Au, Scientific Reports, 8 (2018) 15593.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor for the detection of HTLV-I DNA: a cascade signal amplification strategy integrating λ-exonuclease aided target recycling with hybridization chain reaction and enzyme catalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-M. Shi, G.-C. Fan, X. Tang, Q. Shen, J.-J. Zhu, Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical biosensor for the detection of HTLV-I DNA: a cascade signal amplification strategy integrating λ-exonuclease aided target recycling with hybridization chain reaction and enzyme catalysis, Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 109 (2018) 190-196.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid and label-free electrochemical DNA biosensor based on a facile one-step electrochemical synthesis of rGO–PPy–(l-Cys)–AuNPs nanocomposite for the HTLV-1 oligonucleotide detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Fani, M. Rezayi, H.R. Pourianfar, Z. Meshkat, M. Makvandi, M. Gholami, S.A. Rezaee, Rapid and label‐free electrochemical DNA biosensor based on a facile one‐step electrochemical synthesis of rGO–PPy–(l‐Cys)–AuNPs nanocomposite for the HTLV‐1 oligonucleotide detection, Biotechnology and Applied Biochemistry, 68 (2021) 626-635.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a single-molecule biosensor with an ultra-low background for the simultaneous detection of multiple retroviral DNAs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.-y. Wang, Y.-r. Meng, J. Hu, J.-G. Qiu, C.-y. Zhang, Development of a single-molecule biosensor with an ultra-low background for the simultaneous detection of multiple retroviral DNAs, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, 10 (2022) 5465-5472.
